macro_events.event_summary: Trump told CNBC he expects the U.S. to reach a "great deal" with Iran, signaling de-escalation and potential sanctions relief.
primary_drivers: Lower geopolitical risk premium in oil
Potential Iranian crude re-entering global supply if sanctions ease
Reduced Middle East conflict risk lowering defense demand expectations

beneficiaries.rationale: Southwest is a domestic carrier with no fuel hedges in recent years — most jet-fuel-sensitive major US airline. Lower crude on Iran deal directly expands margin.
Jet fuel is ~20-25% of operating cost. A diplomatic deal pressuring Brent lower flows directly to airline opex within weeks.
Cruise operator with heavy bunker fuel exposure; also benefits from reduced Middle East travel-disruption risk. Double lever on de-escalation.
Commodity chemicals producer where naphtha/ethane feedstock tracks oil. Lower crude compresses input costs while demand unchanged.

losers.rationale: Largest US integrated with significant upstream leverage to Brent. Iranian barrels returning to market (potentially 1-1.5mbpd over time) pressures crude and E&P earnings.
High-beta Permian pure-play E&P with leveraged balance sheet — most oil-price-sensitive large cap. Direct hit from lower crude expectations.
Small-cap E&P with historical Egyptian operations and high oil-price torque; moves hardest on Middle East de-escalation headlines.
Lockheed's missile defense (THAAD, PAC-3) and munitions demand has been fueled by Iran/Israel tensions. Diplomatic track softens the near-term replenishment thesis.
Patriot interceptor demand and Middle East FMS pipeline priced on sustained tension. Peace headline compresses the defense risk-premium trade.
Defense prime leveraged to Middle East threat narrative; typically sells off on Iran de-escalation headlines alongside LMT/RTX as a group trade.

headlines: Trump tells CNBC he expects U.S. to make ‘great deal’ with Iran

macro_events.event_summary: March retail sales posted a 3-year high, partly driven by surging gasoline prices but also showing resilient underlying consumer demand.
primary_drivers: Higher gasoline prices inflating nominal retail sales at gas stations
Resilient consumer spending suggests sticky inflation and pushes back Fed cut expectations
Higher-for-longer rates = stronger USD, higher yields, pressure on duration-sensitive names

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with large US downstream/retail footprint; rising gasoline prices directly boost refining margins and gas-station revenue captured in the retail sales print.
Marathon owns the largest US refining system and Speedway legacy wholesale fuel distribution; direct leverage to higher pump prices feeding the retail sales beat.
Pure-play refiner with branded retail gasoline exposure; crack spreads and retail fuel margins expand with pump price surge cited as driver.
Casey's General Stores — convenience/gas retailer where higher fuel prices lift revenue and in-store traffic holds up given 'consumers aren't showing any quit'.
Resilient consumer narrative supports staples retailers; Walmart also captures fuel station revenue and benefits from trade-down dynamics if gas squeezes discretionary budgets.
Membership warehouse with significant fuel sales; strong consumer print validates traffic trends and Costco's gas pumps capture the price surge.

losers.rationale: Dollar Tree's low-income customer is most squeezed by gasoline price surge — fuel is a bigger share of wallet; despite headline strength, this cohort's discretionary spend compresses.
Dollar General same dynamic — core customer earns <$40k and has already cited gas prices as cause of traffic weakness in recent quarters.
Southwest lacks fuel hedges; surging crude/gasoline complex raises jet fuel costs with limited pass-through given price-sensitive leisure customer.

headlines: Retail sales jump to 3-year high due to surging gas prices, but consumers also aren’t showing any quit

macro_events.event_summary: Steel Dynamics guides to multi-year strong demand citing Trump tariff tailwinds; STLD and Nucor shares break out.
primary_drivers: Tariff-driven import substitution raising domestic steel pricing power
Sustained volume demand outlook from onshoring/infrastructure
Sector re-rating as peers read across from STLD commentary

beneficiaries.rationale: Largest US domestic steel producer, near-pure-play EAF minimill; directly benefits from same tariff-protected pricing and demand backdrop STLD just validated. Already cited in headline as breaking out on read-across.
The company itself — guided to years of strong demand, confirming tariff tailwind to pricing and volumes. Direct earnings impact.

Cleveland-Cliffs is a major US integrated steelmaker heavily levered to domestic auto/flat-rolled pricing; tariff protection disproportionately helps CLF given its higher cost structure vs imports. Highest operating leverage to HRC prices among peers.
Commercial Metals — domestic rebar/long-products minimill, benefits from same import-substitution dynamic and infrastructure demand narrative STLD is calling out.
ArcelorMittal has significant US operations (former AM/NS Calvert and North American segment) that benefit from Section 232-style tariff regime; partial but real exposure.

losers.rationale: Ford is a major US steel buyer; sustained elevated domestic HRC prices from tariffs are a direct COGS headwind on vehicle margins. Auto OEMs are the pay-side of the steel trade.
Same as Ford — large flat-rolled steel consumer with limited ability to import-substitute; tariff-elevated domestic steel prices compress auto margins.
Whirlpool's appliance COGS are steel-heavy; prolonged tariff-supported pricing is a margin headwind, particularly as consumer demand limits passthrough.
Deere uses substantial steel in equipment manufacturing; higher sustained domestic steel prices pressure gross margins absent equivalent price increases.

headlines: Steel Dynamics Sees Strong Demand For Years Amid Trump Tariffs; STLD, Nucor Break Out
